narrative.setting: The Magic School Bus descends deeper into Mr. Edgar's airway. You pass through the pharynx and arrive at a complex structure of cartilage and muscle - the larynx, the voice box. The vocal cords are red and swollen, barely able to vibrate. Mr. Edgar tries to speak but only a hoarse whisper emerges. Ms. Frizzle's voice is gentle: 'Laryngitis. His voice is gone because this incredible instrument is inflamed. We need to understand what 'laryngo-' means and why voice is lost.'
narrative.tension: Mr. Edgar is a teacher who needs his voice to work. Is this acute laryngitis that will resolve, or chronic damage?

scaffolds.narrative: Ms. Frizzle projects the anatomy. 'Larynx - from the Greek larynx, meaning voice box. The organ of phonation. The instrument that makes us human speakers.'
scaffolds.explain: 'LARYNX' = VOICE BOX. The cartilaginous structure in the neck containing the vocal cords (vocal folds). Functions: phonation (voice production), airway protection during swallowing. Located between pharynx (above) and trachea (below).

wrongExplains.0: Throat is the PHARYNX. The larynx is the voice box, located BELOW the pharynx.
wrongExplains.2: Windpipe is the TRACHEA. The larynx is above the trachea - it's the voice box.
wrongExplains.3: Lungs are the organs of gas exchange. The larynx is in the neck, the voice production organ.

scaffolds.narrative: You float between the vocal cords - two bands of tissue stretched across the airway. Normally they vibrate to produce sound. Now they're thick, inflamed, barely moving. 'The vocal cords,' Ms. Frizzle says reverently.
scaffolds.q: The larynx contains which structures critical for voice production?
scaffolds.explain: The larynx contains the VOCAL CORDS (vocal folds) - bands of muscle and mucosa that vibrate when air passes through, creating sound. Also contains cartilages (thyroid, cricoid, arytenoid) that support and adjust vocal cord tension.

wrongExplains.0: Alveoli are in the lungs (gas exchange). The larynx is in the neck, contains vocal cords.
wrongExplains.2: Tonsils are in the pharynx (throat). The larynx contains vocal cords, not tonsils.
wrongExplains.3: Turbinates are in the nasal cavity (nose). The larynx contains vocal cords.

scaffolds.narrative: Air rushes up from the lungs, trying to make the vocal cords vibrate. But they're too swollen, too stiff. Only a raspy whisper emerges. 'Laryngitis,' Ms. Frizzle diagnoses. 'Inflammation of the larynx.'
scaffolds.explain: '-ITIS' = INFLAMMATION. Laryngitis = larynx inflammation. Causes: viral infection (most common), voice overuse, GERD, irritants (smoke). Primary symptom: HOARSENESS or voice loss.

wrongExplains.0: Removal is '-ectomy' (laryngectomy = surgical removal of larynx). '-itis' means inflammation.
wrongExplains.2: Enlargement is '-megaly'. '-itis' specifically means inflammation (swelling, redness, pain).
wrongExplains.3: Paralysis would be 'laryngeal palsy' or 'vocal cord paralysis'. '-itis' means inflammation.

scaffolds.narrative: Ms. Frizzle zooms in on the inflamed vocal cord surface. The delicate mucosa is red, edematous. 'Swelling,' she explains. 'When vocal cords swell, they can't vibrate properly. No vibration, no voice.'
scaffolds.q: Why does laryngitis cause hoarseness or voice loss?
options: Swollen vocal cords cannot vibrate normally
The brain forgets how to speak
scaffolds.explain: Inflamed vocal cords → SWELLING → altered VIBRATION → abnormal sound production. Mild swelling = hoarseness. Severe swelling = aphonia (no voice). The cords are too thick/stiff to vibrate at their normal frequency.

wrongExplains.0: The lungs still produce air flow. The problem is at the VOCAL CORDS, which can't vibrate properly when swollen.
wrongExplains.2: Speech centers are fine. The mechanical INSTRUMENT (vocal cords) is malfunctioning due to swelling.
wrongExplains.3: The airway is open (you can breathe). The vocal cords just can't produce normal sound when inflamed.

scaffolds.narrative: A viral particle - rhinovirus - is replicating in the laryngeal epithelium. 'Acute viral laryngitis,' Ms. Frizzle announces. 'Like the common cold, but targeting the voice box instead of the nose.'
scaffolds.q: The most common cause of acute laryngitis is:
scaffolds.explain: VIRAL infection is the most common cause of ACUTE laryngitis. Viruses: rhinovirus, influenza, parainfluenza, adenovirus. Often accompanies upper respiratory infections (URIs). Self-limited, resolves in 1-2 weeks.

wrongExplains.0: Bacterial laryngitis is rare (though can occur in croup, epiglottitis). VIRAL is most common for acute cases.
wrongExplains.2: Tumors cause CHRONIC hoarseness (weeks-months), not acute laryngitis. Acute is usually viral.
wrongExplains.3: Allergies can cause throat irritation but rarely cause true laryngitis. Viral infection is the primary cause.

scaffolds.narrative: The bus observes Mr. Edgar's history - teaching classes, yelling at a football game, singing karaoke. 'Voice abuse,' Ms. Frizzle notes. 'Overuse causes mechanical trauma to vocal cords, leading to inflammation.'
scaffolds.q: Besides infection, what can cause laryngitis?
scaffolds.explain: Non-infectious causes: (1) VOICE OVERUSE (yelling, singing), (2) GERD (acid reflux irritates larynx), (3) SMOKING/irritants, (4) allergies, (5) chronic cough. These cause mechanical or chemical irritation → inflammation.

wrongExplains.1: Genetic factors aren't a primary cause of laryngitis. It's usually environmental (infection, overuse, irritants).
wrongExplains.2: Hydration helps PREVENT laryngitis. Drinking water doesn't cause it.
wrongExplains.3: Spicy food might worsen GERD-related laryngitis, but isn't a direct cause of vocal cord inflammation.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Acute versus chronic laryngitis,' Ms. Frizzle teaches. 'Acute is days to weeks. Chronic is months. Different causes, different concerns. Chronic hoarseness needs a laryngoscopy - could be cancer.'
scaffolds.q: Chronic laryngitis (hoarseness > 3 weeks) should prompt evaluation for:
options: Vocal cord nodules, polyps, or laryngeal cancer
scaffolds.explain: CHRONIC hoarseness (>3 weeks) requires laryngoscopy to rule out: (1) VOCAL CORD LESIONS (nodules, polyps, cysts), (2) LARYNGEAL CANCER (especially in smokers), (3) vocal cord paralysis, (4) chronic GERD effects. Don't assume it's 'just laryngitis.'

wrongExplains.0: Common cold causes ACUTE laryngitis (days-weeks). Chronic hoarseness needs investigation for serious causes.
wrongExplains.2: Tooth decay affects teeth, not voice. Chronic hoarseness is a laryngeal problem.
wrongExplains.3: Completely unrelated. Chronic hoarseness points to laryngeal pathology.

scaffolds.narrative: A laryngoscope image appears - the doctor is looking directly at Mr. Edgar's vocal cords. 'Laryngoscopy,' Ms. Frizzle explains. 'Direct visualization of the larynx. Essential for chronic hoarseness.'
options: Visual examination of the larynx with a scope
scaffolds.explain: LARYNGOSCOPY = visual examination of the LARYNX using a laryngoscope (flexible or rigid scope). Allows direct viewing of vocal cords, detection of masses, polyps, paralysis, cancer. '-scopy' = examination.

wrongExplains.0: Removal is laryngectomy. Laryngoscopy is EXAMINATION, not surgery.
wrongExplains.2: X-rays don't show vocal cord detail well. Laryngoscopy uses direct visualization with light and camera.
wrongExplains.3: Blood tests detect infection markers. Laryngoscopy is physical examination of the larynx itself.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Treatment,' Ms. Frizzle announces. 'For acute viral laryngitis: voice rest, hydration, humidification. NO antibiotics - it's viral! For chronic: treat the underlying cause.'
scaffolds.q: The primary treatment for acute viral laryngitis is:
scaffolds.explain: Acute VIRAL laryngitis treatment: (1) VOICE REST (whisper or silence), (2) HYDRATION, (3) humidified air, (4) TIME (self-resolves 1-2 weeks). NO antibiotics (it's viral!). Steroids only for severe cases (singers, actors with urgent need).

wrongExplains.0: Antibiotics don't work on VIRUSES. Acute laryngitis is usually viral - antibiotics are inappropriate.
wrongExplains.2: Surgery is for structural problems (nodules, cancer), not acute inflammation. Rest and time are the cure.
wrongExplains.3: Steroids are reserved for special cases (professional voice users). Routine viral laryngitis doesn't need steroids.

scaffolds.narrative: Mr. Edgar rests his voice. The inflammation subsides. Two weeks later, his voice returns to normal. 'Acute viral laryngitis,' Ms. Frizzle confirms. 'Self-limited, as expected. Final question!'
scaffolds.explain: LARYNGITIS = inflammation of the LARYNX (voice box). Causes: viral infection (most common), voice overuse, GERD, smoking. Primary symptom: HOARSENESS or voice loss. Chronic hoarseness needs laryngoscopy to rule out serious causes.

wrongExplains.0: Throat (pharynx) inflammation is PHARYNGITIS (sore throat). Laryngitis is the voice box, below the throat.
wrongExplains.2: Windpipe (trachea) inflammation is TRACHEITIS. Laryngitis is the voice box, above the trachea.
wrongExplains.3: Lung inflammation is pneumonia or pneumonitis. Laryngitis is in the neck - the voice box.
